questions.text: Alkyl halides are considered to be very reactive compounds towards nucleophile because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Please refer to main explanation.

options.text: They have an electrophilic carbon and a good leaving group
options.explanation: Alkyl halides are considered to be very reactive compounds towards nucleophiles because they have an electrophilic carbon and a good leaving group. An electrophilic carbon is a carbon atom that has a partial positive charge. This partial positive charge makes the carbon atom more susceptible to attack by a nucleophile, which is an electron-rich species. A good leaving group is an ion that can easily leave a molecule without causing much disruption to the molecule. A good leaving group is usually a negatively charged ion with a relatively small size. In the case of alkyl halides, the halogen atom is the leaving group. The halogen atom is a good leaving group because it is negatively charged and has a relatively small size. When an alkyl halide reacts with a nucleophile, the nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom, displacing the halogen atom. This reaction is called a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

For example, if a molecule contains a methyl group and a chlorine atom, it would be named as chloromethane.

Halogens are not named as parent names in the nomenclature of alkyl halides. The parent name of an alkyl halide is determined by the number of carbon atoms in the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms that contains the halogen atom. The halogen atom is then treated as a substituent on this chain, and its name is added as a prefix to the name of the alkyl group. For example, if a molecule contains a chlorine atom attached to a chain of 4 carbon atoms, it would be named as 1-chlorobutanol. The parent name is "butane" because it contains 4 carbon atoms and the prefix "chloro" is added to indicate the presence of the chlorine substituent.

Alkyl halides are not coordination complexes, and therefore, the halogen atom is not considered a ligand in the nomenclature of alkyl halides. questions.text: Which of the following is false?

options.text: In P-type substance majority charge carriers are holes
options.explanation: A P-N junction is an interface or a boundary between two semiconductor material types, namely the p-type and the n-type, inside a semiconductor. In a semiconductor, the P-N junction is created by the method of doping. The p-side or the positive side of the semiconductor has an excess of holes as charge carriers, and the n-side, or the negative side has an excess of electrons as charge carriers.

options.text: During forward biasing of a divide width of depletion region decreases
options.explanation: When the p-type is connected to the battery’s positive terminal and the n-type to the negative terminal, then the P-N junction is said to be forward-biased. When the P-N junction is forward biased, the built-in electric field at the P-N junction and the applied electric field are in opposite directions. When both the electric fields add up, the resultant electric field has a magnitude lesser than the built-in electric field. This results in a less resistive and thinner depletion region. The depletion region’s resistance becomes negligible when the applied voltage is large. In silicon, at the voltage of 0.6 V, the resistance of the depletion region becomes completely negligible, and the current flows across it unimpeded.

options.text: Leakage current flows to minority charge carriers
options.explanation: When the P-N junction diode is in negative bias condition, the p-type is connected to the negative terminal while the n-type is connected to the positive terminal of the external voltage. This results in an increase in the potential barrier. Reverse saturation current flows in the beginning as minority carriers are present in the junction. When the applied voltage is increased, the minority charges will have increased kinetic energy which affects the majority charges. This is the stage when the diode breaks down. This may also destroy the diode.
All the given options are true hence, none of them is false.

questions.text: Full wave bridge rectifier uses:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A full wave rectifier cannot be implemented using only one diode. A diode is a semiconductor device that allows current to flow in one direction and blocks it in the opposite direction. For a full wave rectification, which means converting an AC (alternating current) signal into a pulsating DC (direct current) signal, we need to utilize both the positive and negative cycles of the AC signal. A single diode can only rectify either the positive or the negative half-cycle, not both.

A full wave rectifier typically uses two diodes arranged in a bridge configuration called a "bridge rectifier" or "full-wave bridge rectifier." This configuration allows both the positive and negative halves of the AC signal to be rectified, resulting in a pulsating DC output.

A configuration with three diodes cannot produce full wave rectification. While it is possible to design various rectifier circuits with three diodes (such as center-tapped rectifiers), these configurations typically provide half-wave rectification rather than full wave.

A full wave rectifier generally uses four diodes arranged in a bridge configuration (bridge rectifier). These four diodes allow both halves of the AC input signal to be rectified, resulting in a smoother pulsating DC output compared to half-wave rectifiers.

questions.explanation: Full wave bridge rectifier uses four diodes. However, centre tap transformer uses two diodes.

options.explanation: Option (a) "E1, SN2" is not the correct answer. The first step in the E1 (unimolecular elimination) reaction involves the ionization of the leaving group to form a carbocation intermediate. On the other hand, the first step in the SN2 (bimolecular nucleophilic substitution) reaction involves the attack of a nucleophile on the substrate while the leaving group departs. These two reactions have different first steps and mechanisms.

Option (b) "E2, SN1" is not the correct answer either. The first step in the E2 (bimolecular elimination) reaction involves the deprotonation of the substrate by a strong base and the concurrent removal of the leaving group. In contrast, the first step in the SN1 (unimolecular nucleophilic substitution) reaction involves the formation of a carbocation intermediate from the substrate. These two reactions have different first steps and mechanisms.
Option (c) "E2, SN2" is not the correct answer. As discussed earlier, the first steps in E2 and SN2 reactions are different, involving different mechanisms.

Option (d) "E1, SN1" is the correct answer. The first steps in the E1 and SN1 reactions are similar, involving the formation of a carbocation intermediate. In the E1 reaction (unimolecular elimination), the leaving group departs, forming a carbocation intermediate, while in the SN1 reaction (unimolecular nucleophilic substitution), the nucleophile attacks the carbocation intermediate. Both reactions proceed via a carbocation intermediate in their first step.

questions.text: The product in SN2 reaction is formed with?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In an SN2 reaction, a nucleophile attacks an electrophilic carbon atom, causing the carbon to become chiral. The nucleophile attacks from the side opposite the leaving group, causing the configuration of the stereocenter to invert. This inversion of configuration results in the product having the opposite stereochemistry compared to the starting material. The SN2 reaction is stereospecific, meaning that the stereochemistry of the starting material determines the stereochemistry of the product.

Retention configuration refers to a situation in which the product of a reaction has the same stereochemistry as the starting material. In an SN2 reaction, retention configuration occurs when the nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon from the same side as the leaving group. However, this is not a common occurrence in SN2 reactions.

The product in an SN2 reaction is not formed with 50% retention in configuration. In an SN2 reaction, the product will have the opposite stereochemistry as the starting material when there is retention in configuration. However, retention in configuration is not a common occurrence in SN2 reactions.

The product in an SN2 reaction is formed with 100% inversion in configuration. In an SN2 reaction, the nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon from the side opposite to the leaving group, resulting in inversion of the configuration at the stereocenter. Therefore, the product will have the opposite stereochemistry as the starting material.

questions.text: What is the order of SN2 reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In first-order reactions, the rate of the reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of a single reactant. SN2 reactions are not first-order reactions, as they involve two reactants: the nucleophile and the substrate.

In zero-order reactions, the rate of the reaction is independent of the concentration of the reactants. Again, SN2 reactions are not zero-order reactions because their rate depends on the concentration of both the nucleophile and the substrate.
The SN2 reaction is a second-order reaction. This means that the rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of both the nucleophile and the substrate.
Third-order reactions have a rate that is proportional to the product of the concentrations of three reactants. SN2 reactions do not involve three reactants, so they are not third-order reactions.
